summary: Gruber highlights a basic Siri failure: when asked 'What time is it in London?' on Apple Watch and iPhone, Siri returns the time for London, Ontario instead of London, England. He argues this isn't a minor quibble but reveals a fundamental lack of common sense in Siri's design. Making matters worse, Siri on HomePod correctly answers London, England, exposing baffling inconsistency across Apple's own devices. Every competing service — Google, Alexa, DuckDuckGo, Bing — gets it right, leaving Siri as the sole failure.
key_points: Siri on Apple Watch and iPhone interprets 'London' as London, Ontario rather than London, England — a common sense failure
Siri on HomePod gives the correct answer, revealing troubling inconsistency across Apple's own devices
Every competing service (Google, Alexa, DuckDuckGo, Bing) answers the question correctly
The error isn't just wrong — it's slow, compounding the poor experience
Gruber frames this as emblematic of a deeper systemic problem with Siri rather than an isolated bug
money_quotes: Stupid and slow is a heck of a combination.
The right answer is the common sense answer.

If you had a human assistant and asked them 'What's the time in London?' and they honestly thought the best way to answer that question was to give you the time for the nearest London, which happened to be in Ontario or Kentucky, you'd fire that assistant.
You wouldn't fire them for getting that one answer wrong, you'd fire them because that one wrong answer is emblematic of a serious cognitive deficiency that permeates everything they try to do.
You don't have to be particularly smart or knowledgeable to assume that 'London' means 'London England', you just have to not be stupid.
Why in the world would you get a completely different answer to a very simple question based solely on which device answers your question?
At least when most computer systems are wrong they're consistently wrong.
So every other service that tries to answer 'What time is it in London?' gets it right. Only Siri gets it wrong.
key_insight: Siri's failure on a trivially simple question — one every competitor handles correctly — isn't an isolated bug but symptomatic of a deeper lack of common sense that undermines Apple's entire voice assistant strategy.
